basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Ghāshiya — “The Overwhelming Veil” — name of the Resurrection as the veil that falls upon all creation. The sūra is built as a mirror: on one side, debased faces, exhausted, given to drink from a boiling spring (vv. 2-7); on the other, radiant faces, in a lofty Garden, by a flowing spring (vv. 8-16). But the passage from Judgment to the beauty of the Garden leads, surprisingly, to an invitation to look: “Do they not consider how the camel was created, how the heaven was raised, how the mountains were set up, how the earth was spread out?” The warning opens onto the contemplation of near signs. Qushayrī yields several pearls in this sūra. On the image of the camel: at the Garden, the lofty thrones will lower themselves as a camel kneels for its master to mount. On the silent promise of the Garden (“they will hear no idle word there”): “they hear by God — and so no vain word remains there.” And on the flowing springs: “those that flow tomorrow are for those who, today, have eyes flowing with tears — and tomorrow, they will have eyes that gaze in the encounter.” Thus the sūra, opened on the weight of the Day, closes on a heart called to see and to call.

versets.trad: Has the report of the Overwhelming Veil reached you?
versets.commentaire: al-ghāshiya (الغَاشِيَة) — Qushayrī, in one word: “that which wraps entirely.” The root gh-sh-y names the veil that falls, what covers without leaving an exit. It is one of the Quranic names of the Resurrection: not a local scene, but what falls upon all. The sūra opens with a question: “have you heard the report?” — as one warns a friend of a grave matter, wanting him to know before it arrives.

mots.def: atā: to come, to reach; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ghāshiya: active feminine participle of ghashiya; she who veils, covers, wraps entirely. A name of the Resurrection.

versets.trad: That Day, debased faces,
versets.commentaire: khāshiʿa (خَاشِعَة): bowed down, low, debased. The same root as khushūʿ, the deep recollection — a subtle irony: those who did not know khushūʿ in prayer will know it on the Day as humiliation. What one has not learned to give freely, one learns under constraint.

mots.def: khāshiʿ: bowed, recollected; here by constraint, debased.

versets.trad: exhausted, harassed,
versets.commentaire: A verse on several levels. Literal sense: these faces are at work and exhausted by the pain of chastisement. But Qushayrī offers two inward readings that overturn the perspective. (1) “ʿāmila in this world by transgressions, nāṣiba in the next by chastisement.” (2) More striking: “nāṣiba in this world, ʿāmila indeed — but without sincerity, like the deeds of the monks (rahbān, رَهْبَان), and likewise the deeds of the hypocrites.” Spiritual effort without ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاص, purity of intention) ends in the same exhaustion. Qushayrī adds, chilling: “If the bodies today take on the form of obediences while the spirits are deprived of unveilings, the secrets lack the lights of contemplations, and the heart lacks sincerity and truthfulness in its beliefs — that profits nothing and helps nothing: it is precisely what is named ‘at work and exhausted.'”

mots.def: ʿāmil: one who acts, who toils; in the feminine.
nāṣib: one who toils, harassed by effort. Naṣb also means exhaustion.

versets.trad: shall be exposed to a blazing Fire.
versets.commentaire: taṣlā (تَصْلَى): they shall burn. ḥāmiya (حَامِيَة): intensely hot. Qushayrī reports the tradition: “they will be dragged on their faces” — the faces that did not humbly prostrate to the ground come there in spite of themselves.

mots.def: ṣaliya: to burn, to be exposed to fire; feminine imperfect (subject: faces).
ḥāmī: burning, blazing; active participle of ḥamiya.

versets.trad: They shall be given to drink from a boiling spring;
versets.commentaire: āniya (آنِيَة): Qushayrī, in one word: “whose heat has reached its uttermost.” An image of water beyond the threshold a body can endure. The sūra does not linger to describe; it leaves the image to act.

mots.sens: they shall be given to drink
mots.def: saqā in the passive; to give to drink.
mots.gram: passive imperfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: ānī: that has reached its peak (heat, ripeness).

versets.ar: لَّيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِن ضَرِيعٍ
versets.tr: laysa lahum ṭaʿāmun illā min ḍarīʿ
versets.trad: they shall have no food but thorns
versets.commentaire: al-ḍarīʿ (الضَّرِيع): Qushayrī specifies: “a plant that grows in the Ḥijāz, covered with thorns, poisonous — even the beasts do not eat of it.” When they eat of it in the Fire, they will choke — and then they will be given to drink of scalding water (al-zaqqūm). The food no living being accepts becomes, by justice, the portion of those who refused true nourishment.

mots.def: defective verb laysa: not to be.
ḍarīʿ: poisonous thorny plant of the Ḥijāz that even beasts will not eat.

versets.ar: لَّا يُسْمِنُ وَلَا يُغْنِي مِن جُوعٍ
versets.tr: lā yusminu wa-lā yughnī min jūʿ
versets.trad: which neither nourishes them nor stills their hunger.
versets.commentaire: An absolute cruelty: they eat and the hunger remains. This verse is also the negative mirror of a life spent feeding on what does not feed — vanities, distractions, attachments. One can, without knowing it, spend a whole life eating ḍarīʿ.

mots.def: asmana: to fatten, to give flesh.

versets.trad: That day, radiant faces,
versets.commentaire: nāʿima (نَاعِمَة): Qushayrī, in one word: “filled with favour, in grace and freshness (naḍāra, نَضَارَة).” The same root n-ʿ-m as niʿma (favour). The face bears the trace of what the soul has been fed. As the exhausted faces bore vain effort, these faces bear received grace.

mots.def: nāʿim: filled with favour, in grace and freshness; active participle of naʿima.

versets.trad: well-pleased with their striving,
versets.commentaire: li-saʿyihā rāḍiya: well-pleased with their effort. Qushayrī: “because they have found the reward of their effort, and that effort has been accepted.” The same root saʿy as in sūra 92 — the effort that made all the difference among the diverging paths. Here, just effort finds its answer. Contentment does not come from rest: it comes from the effort having been seen.

mots.def: saʿy: effort, tense walk; with feminine suffix pronoun.
rāḍiya: well-pleased, content; active feminine participle.

versets.trad: shall be in a lofty Garden
versets.commentaire: ʿāliya (عَالِيَة): lofty. Qushayrī doubles the image: “lofty in its rank, in its station, in its nobility.” Then he opens the most delicate note: “They are with their bodies in their degrees, but with their spirits with God in the precious intimate conversation (munājāt, مُنَاجَاة) with Him.” The Garden is not only the beauty of the place: it is the place where body and spirit are no longer compelled to be in the same place — the spirit can be with God while the body rests in the Garden.

mots.def: ʿālī: elevated; active participle of ʿalā.

versets.trad: where they will hear no idle word.
versets.commentaire: An overwhelming verse, and Qushayrī's reading more so. lāghiya (لَاغِيَة): vain, idle speech. Why would the Garden be free of it? “Because they hear by God,” Qushayrī answers, “and so no vain word remains there.” And he unfolds a quaternary of spiritual hearing that is one of the most beautiful pages of his commentary: “There are people who hear by God, people who hear for God, people who hear from God.” To hear by God (bi-Llāh): to have no other ear than the one He lends. To hear for God (li-Llāh): to listen only to what suits Him. To hear from God (min Allāh): to receive, in every human word, the echo of a higher word. And he seals it with the famous divine ḥadīth: “I become his hearing by which he hears, and his sight by which he sees.” In the Garden, then, nothing is vain any more — because God Himself hears through His friends.

mots.def: lāghiya: idle, vain speech; active feminine participle of laghā.

versets.trad: Therein is a flowing spring
versets.commentaire: ʿayn jāriya: a spring that flows. The word ʿayn also means eye: flowing springs are, literally, “eyes that flow.” Qushayrī seizes the image in a poignant phrase: “Those springs that will flow tomorrow are for those who have, today, eyes flowing with tears. And tomorrow, they will have — beyond the springs — eyes that gaze in the encounter.” A tear here, a spring there. The same water, at different stages. The one who weeps for God is promised the sight of the Beloved's face.

mots.def: ʿayn: eye, spring; here generic for many springs.
jāriya: active feminine participle of jarā; flowing.

versets.trad: and there too couches uplifted,
versets.commentaire: surur marfūʿa: raised couches (or thrones). This verse prepares a flash in the sūra: if the thrones are so high, how is man to mount them? The answer will come in verse 17, with the image of the camel.

mots.def: surur: plural of sarīr; couches, thrones.
marfūʿ: passive participle of rafaʿa; raised.

versets.trad: cups set out,
versets.commentaire: akwāb (أَكْوَاب): cups without handles, for drink. mawḍūʿa (مَوْضُوعَة): “set out” — meaning: within reach, without need to ask. The Garden is described with this minute care: everything is already there. The beneficiary asks for nothing.

mots.def: akwāb: plural of kūb; handleless cups.
mawḍūʿ: passive participle of waḍaʿa; placed, set down.

versets.trad: cushions aligned,
versets.commentaire: namāriq (نَمَارِق) — Qushayrī, in one word: “cushions spread out.” maṣfūfa: aligned in a row. The Garden has a composition; nothing in it is disordered. Order is one of the forms of beauty.

mots.def: namāriq: plural of numruqa; cushions, pillows.
maṣfūf: passive participle of ṣaffa; arranged in order.

versets.trad: carpets spread.
versets.commentaire: zarābī (زَرَابِي) — Qushayrī: “carpets spread out in various places.” The sūra lingers on the details of an entry room — cups, cushions, carpets, couches — as one prepares the house of a guest one loves. The Garden is not an abstract reward; it is a home prepared.

mots.def: zarābī: plural of zarbiyya; luxurious carpets.
mabthūth: passive participle of bathth; scattered, spread.

versets.ar: أَفَلَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَى الْإِبِلِ كَيْفَ خُلِقَتْ
versets.tr: a-fa-lā yanẓurūna ilā l-ibili kayfa khuliqat
versets.trad: Do they not consider how the camel was created,
versets.commentaire: A verse set apart. After the description of the lofty thrones, a question seemed to spring up: how will the believer mount them? Qushayrī then gives the splendid answer he sets in the verse: “Do they not look at the camel, how it was created? — how, when they want to load it or ride it, it kneels? — well then! those thrones likewise will lower themselves, until the friend (walī, وَلِي) mounts them.” The image is inverted: not the saint rising to the throne, but the throne lowering to him. It is the very movement of the camel kneeling before its master. And behind the image, a spiritual truth: what God reserves for His friends comes to meet them. And Qushayrī widens: “These verses came down to admonish, and to lead, through the creatures, to the perfect power of God. The people were nomads of the desert; they saw nothing but the sky, the earth, the mountains, the camels. He commanded them to look.” Contemplating near signs is the access route given to all.

mots.def: khalaqa in the passive feminine perfect (subject: collective noun).
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: how the heaven was raised,
versets.commentaire: From the camel to the heaven — from the small one touches with the eye to the immense that overarches. The sūra thus traces, in four verses, a ladder of contemplation: one begins with what one knows (the camel, a familiar animal in Arabia), then lifts the eyes. The heaven has no visible pillar: God alone holds it.

mots.def: rafaʿa in the passive feminine perfect.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: how the mountains were set up,
versets.commentaire: nuṣibat: they were set up, as one drives a stake (the same root as nāṣiba in verse 3: standing exhaustion). The Quran elsewhere names the mountains “stakes” (awtād) that stabilise the earth. From the infinite heaven, the gaze descends to the fixed mass — and the order of creation reveals itself to be as much descent as ascent.

mots.def: naṣaba in the passive feminine perfect.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: how the earth was spread out?
versets.commentaire: suṭiḥat: it was spread out as a cloth. Four glances, four directions, and the fourth comes back to our feet. The invitation is complete: what we walk upon is also a sign. The next verse will turn from contemplation to mission.

mots.def: saṭaḥa in the passive feminine perfect; to spread flat.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: So give the Reminder! Your duty is only to remind.
versets.commentaire: dhakkir (ذَكِّرْ): remind. mudhakkir (مُذَكِّر): one who reminds. Qushayrī: “You are not over them a controller (musayṭir); remind, O Muḥammad, of what We have commanded you — that is what We have ordered you.” The prophetic function is precisely delimited: to remind, and only to remind. This verse is also, for anyone who teaches or guides, a word of restraint: your role is to convey, not to constrain.

mots.def: dhakkara: intensive form of dhakara; to remind.
mudhakkir: active participle of dhakkara; one who reminds.

versets.trad: You are not appointed to control them.
versets.commentaire: musayṭir (مُصَيْطِر): controller, overseer, dominator. The word is rare in the Quran. The word is very firm: the Prophet is not that controller. And if he himself is not, all the more so his successors are not. The verse founds, in the Quran itself, the limit of preaching: one can call, one cannot constrain.

mots.def: musayṭir: one who controls by force.

versets.trad: As for the one who turns away and disbelieves,
versets.commentaire: The exception comes: the one who has turned away and disbelieved. The same pair of verbs — tawallā wa-kafara — as in sūra 92, 16. The typical denial: one turns the back and denies.

versets.trad: God shall chastise him with the greatest chastisement.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “The one who turned from faith and disbelieved — God will chastise him with eternity in the Fire.” The “greatest chastisement” is the one that lasts; the chastisements of this world, beside it, are brief.

mots.def: ʿadhdhaba: to chastise; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: Truly, to Us is their return!
versets.commentaire: iyāb (إِيَاب): the return. The same grammar as in verse 8 of sūra 96: to your Lord is the return. It is the constant horizon: whatever men do, the motion of every life is a return. None settles definitively elsewhere.

mots.def: iyāb: return; with plural suffix pronoun.

versets.trad: Then upon Us shall their reckoning be.
versets.commentaire: ḥisāb (حِسَاب): the reckoning. Qushayrī, in one word: “We requite them according to good and evil.” The sūra, which opened on the ghāshiya, the veil that covers all, closes on this reckoning which is the reverse of the veil. What was hidden shall be revealed; what was scattered shall be counted; what seemed vain shall be acknowledged. And all this, the verse says, is upon Us — God Himself takes the reckoning in hand. It is, strangely, a consolation for whoever loves justice: no one will take care of His accounting in His stead.
